# LinkedIn Growth Engine — Content Generation Rulebook

**Version:** 1.5 (public release)
**Status:** Canonical content rules
**Reviewed by:** Two independent LinkedIn experts (10yr + 20yr experience). All corrections applied.
**Purpose:** The definitive rules for the LinkedIn Growth Engine content generation system. Every post generated by this skill must pass these checks.
**Language scope:** This rulebook is written for English-language content. The banned vocabulary (Section 2.2), em dash rules (Section 2.7), and broetry patterns (Section 2.3) are English-specific and may not transfer to other languages. Internationalization is out of scope for v1.

---

## How This Document Works

This rulebook has eleven sections:

1. **Ground Rules** — non-negotiable content integrity principles (5 rules)
2. **Anti-Pattern Blacklist** — specific words, phrases, and structures to never use (8 subsections including reshare rules)
3. **Quality Gates** — validation checks every post must pass before scheduling (9 gates, split into per-post and batch-level passes)
4. **Engagement Architecture** — closing rules, see-more engineering, dwell time, posting cadence, pillar enforcement, timely content, post-editing rules, and proactive comment rules (11 subsections)
5. **Format & First Comment** — format diversity, image strategy, and companion content (4 subsections)
6. **What Good Looks Like** — signals of authentic, high-performing content
7. **Hashtag Strategy** — selection, placement, and rotation rules (3 subsections)
8. **Performance Feedback Loop** — monthly analysis, weight adjustment, and re-engagement rules
9. **Implementation Notes** — system prompt structure, two-pass validation pipeline, and batch generation
10. **Competitive Intelligence Framework** — niche analysis, transferable pattern extraction, and strategy calibration (3 subsections)
11. **Reshare Strategy** — reshare scouting, commentary rules, and candidate selection (3 subsections)

The validation pipeline runs in two passes: per-post (Gates 1-3, 5-6, 8, 8b) and batch-level (Gates 4, 7, 9).

**This document is the single source of truth.** The thin module wrappers in `../modules/` point back to sections here. If a wrapper and this Rulebook ever disagree, the Rulebook wins.

---

## What this rulebook is optimizing for

Every rule in this document serves a specific definition of "good." Read it before you read the rules — the rules only make sense in the context of what they're trying to achieve.

**A "good" LinkedIn post, by this rulebook's standards, is:**

- **Voice-locked** to a real human's actual writing register, calibrated from reference samples
- **Specific over general** — real numbers, real names (when shareable), real timeframes, real situations
- **Earned vulnerability over manufactured vulnerability** — failures that still hurt, not failures with tidy redemption arcs
- **Optimized for dwell time and meaningful comments** (15+ words), not raw reaction counts
- **Building topical authority over 60-90 days** through pillar consistency, not chasing virality
- **Designed to attract inbound DMs from a specific ICP**, not to maximize follower count

**This rulebook is NOT optimizing for:**

- Virality, follower count growth, or "going viral"
- The "thought leader" aesthetic (polished closings, motivational poster lines, perfectly-balanced paragraphs)
- Engagement bait ("Comment YES if you agree", "Tag a friend")
- Short-term algorithm hacks
- Corporate brand voice or company-page content
- Mass-market or B2C audiences

If your definition of "good" is different from the one above, **fork the rulebook and adjust it.** Don't follow rules that conflict with your goals — the rules will frustrate you and the output will land flat. Every rule below is defensible *within this editorial position*. Outside it, individual rules may not apply.

This isn't a universal LinkedIn rulebook. It's *our* LinkedIn rulebook, and we're naming the position so you can decide whether to opt in.

---

## 1. Ground Rules (Non-Negotiable)

### Rule 1: Never fabricate stories, quotes, or scenarios
If a post uses a story, a conversation, a meeting, or a specific example, you provided it. The AI shapes and frames it. The AI does not invent it.

If a post needs a story you haven't provided, the system should output: `[STORY_NEEDED: description of what to ask you]`

### Rule 2: Never invent data or statistics
Numbers come from your real experience or from verified sources with attribution. No "studies show" without an actual study. No "X% of companies" without a source.

If a post would benefit from data you haven't provided, use framing that doesn't require a number: "I've seen this pattern across multiple projects" instead of "73% of businesses face this."

### Rule 3: Opinions and frameworks are fine
The AI can generate original takes, contrarian opinions, how-to frameworks, and thought leadership. These come from your strategy and positioning. "Most redesigns fail because..." is a perspective, not a fabrication.

### Rule 4: When a post needs a story, prompt for one
Instead of inventing one, flag it for review: "This post would be stronger with a real example. Have you had a situation where [relevant scenario]? Give me 2-3 sentences."

### Rule 5: Never contradict your established positioning
Every post must align with your content pillars and positioning from your brand profile. If your positioning is "fractional design leader for complex SaaS products," the system must not generate posts about general startup tips, logo design, or topics outside your declared expertise lane. The LinkedIn algorithm rewards topical consistency over 60-90 days and penalizes topic hopping. Off-pillar posts should only be generated when you explicitly request them.

---

## 2. Anti-Pattern Blacklist

### 2.1 Banned Opening Phrases

Never start a post with any of these:

- "In today's fast-paced digital landscape..."
- "In today's ever-evolving world..."
- "As technology continues to evolve..."
- "I am thrilled to announce..."
- "I am pleased to announce..."
- "Let me tell you a story..."
- "Here are my three takeaways..."
- "Nobody is coming to save you."
- "I learned this the hard way." (unless you literally said this)
- "The game has changed."
- "Stay humble. Stay hungry."
- "Consistency is the real flex."
- "Trust the process."
- "Your habits determine your future."
- "Most people [wrong thing]. Successful people [right thing]."
- "Unpopular opinion:" (followed by a safe take)
- "I quit my [impressive job]. Here's why."
- "X years ago, I [failure]. Now [success]."

### 2.2 Banned Vocabulary

These words have seen 1,000%+ usage spikes since ChatGPT's launch and are near-certain AI signals. Never use them.

**Tier 1 — Absolute ban (strongest AI signal):**
delve, tapestry, paradigm, harness, unlock, realm, meticulous(ly), unparalleled, underscore, showcase, pivotal, testament, commendable, groundbreaking, revolutionize, transformative

**Tier 2 — Strong ban:**
leverage, synergy, innovative, game-changer, cutting-edge, landscape, intricate, crucial, vibrant, foster, holistic, garner, accentuate, pioneering, trailblazing, unleash, versatile, redefine, seamless, optimize, robust, breakthrough, empower, streamline

**Tier 3 — Use sparingly (max once per month across all posts):**
elevate, adaptive, effortless, data-driven, proactive, visionary, disruptive, reimagine, agile, democratize, accelerate, state-of-the-art, dynamic, future-proof, next-generation

### 2.3 Banned Structural Patterns

**Broetry (empty single-sentence paragraph stacking):**
```
❌ One sentence.
❌
❌ Another sentence.
❌
❌ A third that adds nothing.
❌
❌ Dramatic pause.
```
Extreme broetry (4+ consecutive single-sentence paragraphs carrying no new information) is penalised by LinkedIn's 2026 algorithm. However, short paragraphs (1-2 sentences) are fine when each one carries a distinct idea. The test is not "is this a single sentence?" but **"does this paragraph advance the argument?"** If removing it loses meaning, keep it. If not, merge it. Mixed rhythm (some short, some 2-3 sentence paragraphs) reads more naturally than forcing every paragraph to be multi-sentence. Mobile readability demands short paragraphs, dense blocks of text kill dwell time even when the content is strong.

**The false opposition:**
- ⚠️ "It's not X, it's Y."
- ⚠️ "The problem isn't X. The problem is Y."
- Allowed when genuinely true (max once per post, not in consecutive posts). Banned as a structural crutch (appearing 2+ times in a single post, or in 3+ posts in the same month). This pattern works when it reframes a real misconception. It fails when it's used to manufacture a contrarian angle.

**Snappy triads:**
- ❌ "Fast, efficient, and reliable."
- ❌ "Simple, powerful, and scalable."

**Unearned profundity:**
- ❌ "But here's the thing..."
- ❌ "Something shifted..."
- ❌ "Here's what I know..."
- ❌ "And that's when I realized..."

**Template scaffold:**
- ❌ Hook → 3 bullet points → conclusion → question (the exact pattern AI detectors flag)

**Therapist mode:**
- ❌ "You're not alone."
- ❌ "Give yourself permission to..."
- ❌ "I want to be honest with you."

**Manufactured vulnerability:**
- ❌ Admitting a past failure that has a tidy resolution where the writer comes out looking good
- ✅ Admitting a failure that's still messy, uncomfortable, or unresolved — IF you actually experienced it

**Borrowed authority:**
- ❌ Dropping famous names (Bezos, Musk, Jobs, Buffett) to borrow credibility
- ✅ Referencing real people you have actually worked with or learned from

### 2.4 Reshare/Repost Rules

Reshares with commentary are a valid content type and can drive engagement, especially for "Grow audience" and "Thought leadership" goals. But they require different rules than original posts.

**Good reshare rules:**
- The commentary must add a genuine perspective, not just "Great post!" or "Had to share this"
- Minimum commentary length: 50 words (enough to constitute its own micro-post)
- The commentary should take a position on the original content: agree with a specific reason, disagree with a nuance, or extend the original idea with your own experience
- The commentary must be able to stand alone if the reshared content were removed. If it only makes sense paired with the original, it's too dependent.

**Reshare limits (canonical — this section governs):**
- Maximum 2 reshares per month. Section 11 provides the comprehensive reshare strategy treatment.
- Never reshare two posts in the same week
- Reshares do not count toward pillar quotas, they are additional to the planned batch

**Reshare anti-patterns:**
```
❌ "Had to share this" (lazy, no value added)
❌ "This." or "💯" with no commentary
❌ Resharing viral content just for reach with a generic take
❌ Resharing content from competitors without acknowledging it
```

### 2.5 Banned CTA Patterns

These are now algorithmically suppressed by LinkedIn:

- ❌ "Agree or disagree?"
- ❌ "Comment YES if you agree!"
- ❌ "Like for Part 2"
- ❌ "Follow me for more"
- ❌ "Tag a friend who needs this!"
- ❌ "What would you add? Drop it in the comments."
- ❌ "Thoughts?"
- ❌ "This." (reshare with no commentary)

### 2.6 Banned Formatting

- ❌ Emoji at the start of every bullet point (✅, 💡, 📊 lists)
- ❌ Rocket emoji (🚀) anywhere — the definitive AI signature
- ❌ Emoji used as section dividers
- ❌ Unicode bold/italic styling (𝗯𝗼𝗹𝗱, 𝘪𝘵𝘢𝘭𝘪𝘤)
- ❌ Decorative Unicode characters (→ × ✦) with no semantic function
- ❌ Three or more trailing hashtags
- ❌ External links in the post body (25-40% reach reduction — put in first comment)

### 2.7 Em Dash Rule

Em dashes — like this — have tripled in AI content and are now a strong tell. Rules:
- Default maximum: 2 em dashes per post
- The system should count em dashes in your reference samples and calibrate automatically. If your natural writing averages 3+ em dashes per post, allow up to 3. If your samples have zero, enforce zero.
- Prefer commas, periods, or restructured sentences when the em dash adds no clarity

### 2.8 AI Sentence-Level Tells

These are not vocabulary issues but structural patterns that read as AI-generated even when the individual words are fine.

**"The gap between X and Y":**
- ❌ "The gap between a demo and a real project is..."
- ❌ "The gap between strategy and execution is where..."
- This construction is overused in AI-generated content. Rewrite as two separate observations or name the problem directly without the framing device.

**Polished parallel closings:**
- ❌ "X to build fast, Y to build right."
- ❌ "Less process, more progress."
- Symmetrical phrase pairs as closing lines are a strong AI structural tell. If the closing sounds like it belongs on a motivational poster, rewrite it.

**Restating the author's point as your opening (comments):**
- ❌ "The UX reinvention point is the key one."
- ❌ "The speed of building a demo is real."
- Starting a comment by echoing what the author said adds no value. The reader already read the post. Open with your own observation, extension, or challenge.

**"The X point / part / piece":**
- ❌ "The trust part is what most teams miss."
- ❌ "The execution piece is where it breaks down."
- This construction labels someone else's idea rather than engaging with it. Name the actual thing instead of wrapping it in "the X part."

**Negation-reframe opener ("Not X. Because Y." / "Not because X. Because Y."):**
- ❌ "Not because the model is wrong. Because nobody designed for the moment it gets it wrong."
- ❌ "Not because they lack talent. Because nobody gave them the right brief."
- ❌ "It's not a design problem. It's a clarity problem."
- This is a structural tell where the writer negates an obvious explanation and reframes with a "deeper" one. AI models default to this construction because it creates instant contrast with minimal effort. Once per post maximum, never in the opening two lines, and never combined with another false opposition in the same post. If you spot two negation-reframes in a single post, rewrite one as a direct statement.

**Self-referential comments:**
- ⚠️ "In the projects I work on..." / "What I notice on the teams I work with..." / "The best client relationships I've had..."
- Every comment redirecting to your own experience reads as self-promotion. Allowed max once per session (5 comments). The other comments should add value to the conversation without referencing yourself. An expert's perspective shows through the quality of the observation, not by citing their own work.

---

## 3. Quality Gates

Every generated post must pass ALL of these checks before being scheduled. The validation pipeline runs these automatically after generation.

### Gate 1: Fabrication Check
- Does the post contain quoted dialogue? → Flag unless you provided it
- Does the post reference a specific person, meeting, or event? → Flag unless you provided it
- Does the post cite a statistic or percentage? → Verify source exists or flag
- Does the post contain "I was on a call..." or "Last week a client..." → Flag unless you provided the story

### Gate 2: Anti-Pattern Scan
- Contains any Tier 1 or Tier 2 banned word? → Rewrite
- Starts with a banned opening phrase? → Rewrite
- Uses broetry (4+ consecutive single-sentence paragraphs where any paragraph adds no new information)? → Restructure
- Contains a banned CTA pattern? → Replace with approved closing from your voice profile
- Contains 🚀 or emoji-prefixed bullet lists? → Remove
- Exceeds your calibrated em dash limit (default: 2)? → Rewrite
- Has three or more trailing hashtags in the post body? → Remove (first comment hashtags are separate, see Section 7.1)

### Gate 3: Voice Match Check
- Does the formality level match your voice profile?
- Does the hook style match your selected default for this pillar?
- Does the closing style match your selected default for this pillar?
- Is the sentence rhythm consistent with your profile? (short/mixed/flowing)
- Are there words from your "always avoid" list? → Rewrite
- Could this post have been written by any of 100 other people in your industry? → If yes, add something specific

### Gate 4: Structural Variety Check (batch-level, not per-post)
- Do any two posts in this batch start with the same hook pattern? → Vary
- Do any two posts in the same week cover the same pillar? → Reassign
- Are all posts the same length (±20%)? → Vary some shorter, some longer
- Do all posts follow the same structure (hook → body → close)? → Vary at least 2-3

### Gate 5: LinkedIn Algorithm Alignment
- Post contains an external link in the body? → Move to first comment suggestion
- Post uses more than 3 hashtags? → Reduce to 0-2
- Post is under 50 words? → Flag (too short for dwell time)
- Post is over 3,000 characters? → Flag (may lose mobile readers)
- Post contains engagement bait language? → Rewrite

### Gate 6: Engagement Architecture Check
- Does the post end with a statement and nothing else? → Add a closing (Type A, B, or C from Section 4.1)
- Read the first 210 characters only. Is the thought complete? → Rewrite opening to create open loop (Section 4.2)
- Is the post under 150 words? → Flag (insufficient dwell time)
- Does the post include a first comment? → Generate one per Section 5.2 rules
- Is at least 1 reply template included? → Generate per Section 4.4 rules

### Gate 7: Format Diversity Check (batch-level)
- Are all posts in the monthly batch text + image? → Convert at least 2 to carousel and 1 to multi-image or poll (Section 5.1)
- Has the same closing type been used 3+ times in a row? → Vary per Section 4.1 batch rule
- Are there more than 2 soft-CTA first comments in any given week? → Replace excess with other first comment types

### Gate 8: The Colleague Test (enforceable check)
> "Could someone who knows you personally tell that AI assisted with this post? If the post could have come from any of 1,000 other people in your industry, it's commodity content."

**Machine-verifiable floor (automated):** The post must contain at least one proper noun + one number + one timeframe. This is the minimum automated specificity check.

**Human review aspiration (Colleague Test):** For human-reviewed content, also apply the full Colleague Test below. Both gates co-exist: machine check as minimum, Colleague Test as quality aspiration.

**Enforcement criteria (must pass at least 2 of 3):**
- The post contains at least one detail that only you would know (a specific scenario, a number from your work, a named framework you created)
- The post takes a position that could be disagreed with, OR applies a general concept to a specific context (industry, company size, stage) rather than stating it generically. How-to and framework posts (Type C closing) are exempt from the "disagreeable" test but must still pass the specificity test.
- The post uses at least one phrase or speech pattern from your voice profile reference samples

If fewer than 2 criteria pass: inject a specific detail from your story bank, strengthen the opinion to take a real stance, or replace a generic phrase with a signature expression from your voice profile.

### Gate 8b: AI Detection Check (pre-publish, recommended)

Run the final post text through an external AI detection tool before publishing. This catches structural patterns (balanced paragraphs, synthetic transitions, overly clean narrative arcs) that the internal gates may miss.

**Recommended tools (free, no signup):**
- GPTZero (gptzero.me) — most accurate overall, highlights flagged sentences
- Copyleaks (copyleaks.com/ai-content-detector) — 25K character limit free
- QuillBot AI Detector (quillbot.com/ai-content-detector) — clean comparative results

**Rules:**
- If the detector scores the post as >60% AI-generated, rewrite the flagged sentences. Focus on: adding irregularity to sentence rhythm, breaking overly balanced paragraph structure, replacing smooth transitions with more abrupt shifts.
- If the detector scores <40% AI-generated, pass. The goal is not 0%, it's "a human reader wouldn't notice."
- This is a sanity check, not a hard gate. Detectors have false positives. If the post passes Gates 3 and 8 (voice match + colleague test) but flags on detection, use judgment.
- For comments and reshare commentary: not required (too short for reliable detection).

### Gate 9: Topic Consistency Check (batch-level)
Cross-reference this post's topic against your content pillars and the last 30 days of published posts.

- Does this post fall within one of your declared content pillars? → If not, flag with: `[OFF-PILLAR: topic X is outside declared pillars. Publish anyway? Y/N]`
- Are more than 20% of this month's posts outside declared pillars? → Hard fail. Rewrite the excess to fit a pillar or replace.
- Has the same narrow sub-topic appeared more than twice in the last 14 days? → Flag for topic fatigue. Vary the angle or delay.

**Why this matters:** LinkedIn's algorithm builds a topical authority signal over 60-90 days. Accounts that stay on-topic consistently get distributed to a wider audience within their niche. Topic hopping resets this signal.

---

## 4. Engagement Architecture

These rules govern how posts are structured to drive comments, dwell time, saves, and reach. They are parameterized by your **LinkedIn Goal** (defined in your brand profile), because different goals require different engagement mechanics.

### 4.1 Closing Rules (Mandatory)

Every post must end with one of these closing types. Statement-only closings (where the post ends with a conclusion and nothing else) are prohibited — they close the conversation before it starts.

**Closing Type A: Genuine Question**
A question the reader has a personal answer to. Not rhetorical. Not "What do you think?" (too vague). The question should be specific enough that someone can answer in 2-3 sentences from their own experience.

```
✅ "Where was the real bottleneck hiding in your product the last time conversion felt stuck?"
✅ "What did you stop doing that actually grew your business?"
✅ "How many features are on your pricing page right now?"
❌ "Thoughts?" (too vague, algorithmically suppressed)
❌ "Agree or disagree?" (engagement bait)
❌ "What would you add?" (generic)
```

**Closing Type B: Incomplete Statement**
A position stated clearly enough to invite disagreement, or a tension left deliberately unresolved. The reader should feel pulled to add their perspective.

```
✅ "I still don't know if that was the right call."
✅ "The industry is moving this way. Most people aren't ready for what that means."
✅ "Maybe I'm wrong about this one. But I've seen it too many times to ignore."
```

**Closing Type C: Practical Nudge**
A specific, actionable suggestion the reader can try immediately. Works best for how-to and framework posts. The action itself becomes the conversation starter ("I tried this and...").

```
✅ "Next time your conversion numbers feel stuck, look one step past where the drop happens."
✅ "Open your pricing page right now. Count the features. If it's more than 5, that's your answer."
```

**How your LinkedIn Goal affects closing selection:**

| LinkedIn Goal | Preferred closing types | Rationale |
|---------------|------------------------|-----------|
| Inbound leads | A (question) + C (nudge) | Questions start DM conversations. Nudges demonstrate expertise. |
| Thought leadership | A (question) + B (incomplete) | Questions invite debate. Incomplete statements provoke commentary. |
| Grow audience | A (question) | Questions maximize comment count, which drives reach to non-followers. |
| Attract investment | B (incomplete) + C (nudge) | Investors don't comment publicly. Incomplete statements signal depth. Nudges show operational thinking. |
| Employer brand | A (question) | "What's your experience with X?" invites candidates to self-identify. |
| Speaking/media | B (incomplete) | Unresolved positions attract event organizers looking for opinionated speakers. |
| Launch support | C (nudge) + A (question) | Nudges drive trial. Questions drive word-of-mouth. |
| Community growth | A (question) | Questions are the community-building mechanism. |
| Custom goal | A (question) + B (incomplete) | Default fallback. Questions are the safest universal driver. Adjust after first month's data. |

**Batch rule:** In any 4-post week, use at least 2 different closing types. Never use the same closing type 3 times in a row.

### 4.2 "See More" Engineering (Mandatory)

LinkedIn displays the first 2-3 lines (~210 characters on mobile, ~280 on desktop) before truncating behind "see more." The algorithm heavily weights whether users click it. Every post must be structured so the visible portion creates an open loop that cannot be resolved without clicking.

**Rules:**
- The first paragraph must create curiosity, tension, or an incomplete story
- If the hook + first visible line delivers a complete thought, rewrite — the reader has no reason to expand
- Place the most surprising or tension-bearing element at the truncation point, not before it
- Never put the conclusion or lesson in the first 3 lines

**Patterns that force the click:**
```
✅ Story hook: "A client came to us last year convinced their homepage was the problem." [Reader needs to know: was it the problem?]
✅ Contrarian hook: "I sat in a pitch last month where the agency presented 3 homepage concepts. Beautiful work." [Reader needs to know: what went wrong?]
✅ Number hook: "43% of users who signed up never completed setup." [Reader needs to know: why, and what fixed it]
❌ Complete hook: "Most design conversations start in the wrong place. Here's how to fix that." [Reader already has the gist — no pull to expand]
```

**Testing rule:** Read only the first 210 characters of the post. Ask: "Would I click 'see more'?" If the answer is "I already get it," rewrite the opening.

### 4.3 Dwell Time Rules

Dwell time (how long a reader spends on the post) is LinkedIn's strongest quality signal. Meaningful comments (15+ words) > saves > all comments > reactions in algorithmic weight, and dwell time drives all three.

**Rules:**
- Posts under 100 words rarely generate meaningful dwell time. Minimum target: 150 words for text posts.
- Posts over 500 words need exceptional pacing to hold attention. Use sparingly (max 2 per month).
- Sweet spot: 200-350 words. Long enough for depth, short enough to finish.
- Use paragraph breaks to create visual pacing. Dense blocks of text reduce dwell time even when the content is good.
- Place the strongest insight or most surprising detail in the middle third of the post, not the beginning or end. This rewards readers who stay past the fold.

### 4.4 Conversation Continuation Rules

A post's reach compounds when the author replies to comments. These rules govern the AI-generated reply suggestions that accompany each post.

**For every post, generate at least 1 pre-written reply template. Recommended: 3 templates covering different comment types.**

1. **The "great point, and..." reply** — For substantive comments. Acknowledge the commenter's point, then extend the conversation with a follow-up thought or question. Never just "Great point!" or "Thanks for sharing!"
2. **The "here's the nuance" reply** — For comments that oversimplify or disagree. Add a layer of detail without being defensive. "You're right that X, and there's an interesting wrinkle — [specific detail]."
3. **The "question back" reply** — For short or surface-level comments. Ask a genuine follow-up question to draw the commenter into a deeper exchange. "Curious — have you seen that play out differently in [their industry]?"

**Reply tone must match the post's voice profile.** A casual post with formal replies breaks trust.

**Goal-specific reply guidance:**

| LinkedIn Goal | Reply recommendation | Notes |
|---------------|---------------------|-------|
| Inbound leads | Reply to every comment within 1 hour. Prioritize commenters who match your ICP. | A reply chain = a public sales conversation. |
| Thought leadership | Reply with additional depth or a counterpoint. | Shows you have more to say than the post contained. |
| Grow audience | Reply quickly to maximize comment count visibility. | Speed matters more than depth for reach. |
| Employer brand | Reply warmly, mention team culture when relevant. | Candidates are reading the tone of your replies. |
| Custom goal | Reply to every comment within 24 hours. | Default guidance. Responsiveness always helps. |

### 4.5 Hook-to-Close Coherence (Mandatory)

The hook creates a tension. The body explores it. The close must resolve that tension AND open a new thread (the question or incomplete statement). If the hook and close are thematically disconnected, the reader feels bait-and-switched.

**Rules:**
- The closing question or statement must be thematically linked to the hook's promise. A post that opens with "a homepage problem" cannot close with "what's your experience with pricing pages?" unless the body explicitly bridges from one to the other.
- Test: read only the first line and the last line. Does the last line feel like a natural continuation of the first? If the two could belong to different posts, rewrite the close.
- The close should feel like it grew from the post, not like it was appended to generate engagement.

**Examples:**
```
✅ Hook: "A client went from 3% conversion to 9%."
   Close: "What's the boring fix that actually moved your numbers?"
   (Bridge: the post is about boring fixes driving conversion)

❌ Hook: "A client went from 3% conversion to 9%."
   Close: "What's your biggest hiring mistake?"
   (No connection — the reader feels tricked)
```

### 4.6 Visual Separation of Closing (Mandatory)

The closing question, incomplete statement, or practical nudge must be visually separated from the preceding paragraph. It should stand alone as the final element of the post, with white space above it.

**Rules:**
- The closing must be its own paragraph, not the last sentence of a longer paragraph
- There must be a line break between the final body paragraph and the closing
- If using a Type A closing (question), the question should be the dominant closing element. A brief framing sentence after the question is acceptable if it adds warmth or specificity (e.g., "I'd love to hear what worked.")
- If using a Type C closing (nudge), the nudge should be 1-2 sentences max, isolated

**Why:** Posts where the question is buried inside a closing paragraph get fewer comments. Visual isolation signals "this is for you to answer" and lowers the friction to comment.

### 4.7 Posting Cadence (Default Rules)

These are defaults you can override.

**Frequency:**
- Default: 4 posts per week (data-driven day selection, with Tue-Thu + optional as fallback)
- Minimum for algorithm consistency: 3 posts per week
- Maximum before quality dilution: 5 posts per week
- Weekend posts are disabled by default unless your engagement data shows weekend performance above weekday average

**Timing:**
- Default slot: 8:00-10:00 AM in your timezone
- Secondary slot: 12:00-2:00 PM (lunch engagement window)
- After 30 days of data, shift to your audience's peak hours
- Never schedule two posts within 18 hours of each other (cannibalization)

**Posting window by target geography:**

| Target audience | Optimal posting time (local to audience) |
|----------------|----------------------------------------|
| US East Coast | 7:30-9:30 AM ET |
| US West Coast | 8:00-10:00 AM PT |
| UK/Europe | 8:00-10:00 AM GMT/CET |
| Australia/NZ | 8:00-10:00 AM AEST/NZST |
| Global mix | 8:00 AM in your timezone (compromise) |

### 4.8 Content Pillar Enforcement (Batch-Level)

Each monthly batch must follow a pillar distribution aligned with your brand profile. If you haven't set explicit pillar weights, use these defaults:

| Pillar type | Default weight | Purpose |
|-------------|---------------|---------|
| Expertise / Authority | 40% (6-7 posts/month) | Demonstrate domain knowledge |
| Personal story / Journey | 30% (5 posts/month) | Build connection and trust |
| Engagement / Conversation | 20% (3-4 posts/month) | Spark dialogue, polls, questions |
| Promotional / CTA | 10% (1-2 posts/month) | Direct business value |

**Rules:**
- No single pillar may exceed 50% of the monthly batch
- The promotional pillar must never exceed 15% (max 2 posts out of 16). More than that turns the feed into a sales channel.
- Pillar assignments must be distributed across weeks, not clustered (no "story week" or "expertise week")
- If you provide more stories than the 30% allows, carry surplus to next month's content bank rather than overweighting

**Voice modifiers by pillar:**

Your base voice profile stays constant, but each pillar type naturally calls for slight adjustments. These are not overrides, they are nudges within your existing range.

| Pillar | Voice adjustment |
|--------|-----------------|
| Expertise / Authority | Slightly more precise language. Shorter sentences. Numbers and specifics preferred. Confidence level at the top of your range. |
| Personal story / Journey | Slightly more conversational. Longer, flowing sentences allowed. Vulnerability and uncertainty are assets. First person dominant. |
| Engagement / Conversation | Most casual. Questions and direct address. Shorter posts. Informal markers ("honestly," "look") increased. |
| Promotional / CTA | Matches the expertise pillar but with a clear problem-solution arc. Never reads as a sales pitch, reads as an offer of help. |

These adjustments should be subtle enough that all posts still sound like the same person, just in different modes.

- **Note:** Pillar percentages must be recalculated based on your actual posting frequency, not the 16-post default. At 3 posts/week (12-13/month), Promotional at 10% = 1 post. At 5 posts/week (20/month), Promotional at 10% = 2 posts. The percentages are the rule, the absolute counts flex.

### 4.9 Trending / Timely Content Insertion

The monthly batch is pre-planned, but real-world events happen. You need a mechanism to insert timely posts without breaking the batch structure.

**Rules:**
- You can request an unscheduled post outside the batch at any time ("something just happened in my industry, I want to post about it")
- Timely posts still pass all per-post quality gates (Pass 1) but are exempt from batch-level gates (Pass 2) since they weren't part of the plan
- A timely post replaces the next scheduled post in the batch (it doesn't add to the weekly count). If you post 4x/week and insert a timely post on Wednesday, the planned Wednesday post moves to the following week or is dropped.
- Timely posts should still align with your content pillars. A trending AI story is relevant for a product design consultant who talks about AI. A trending sports story is not.
- Timely insertions capped at 30% of monthly posts (e.g., 5 out of 16 at 4x/week). More than that means the batch planning is misaligned with your actual content needs, and the strategy should be revisited.

### 4.10 Post-Editing Rules

LinkedIn allows editing posts after publication. Edits can recover low performers, but heavy edits have consequences.

**What can be edited (safe):**
- Fixing typos, spelling, and grammar
- Adding a closing question to a post that launched without one (the single most effective recovery action)
- Adding a line break for readability
- Removing a hashtag that's drawing off-topic engagement

**What should not be edited (risky):**
- Rewriting the hook (the algorithm indexed the original, changing it creates a mismatch with who already saw it)
- Changing the topic or core argument (people who commented on the original now look disconnected from the post)
- Adding more than 100 words of new content (LinkedIn may re-evaluate the post, and heavy edits can suppress distribution)
- Removing content that someone already commented on (breaks the comment thread's context)

**Timing:** Edits within the first 2 hours are low-risk. Edits after 24 hours are only worthwhile for typo fixes. Don't edit a post that's more than 48 hours old unless it contains a factual error.

### 4.11 Proactive Comment Rules (Pre-Post Engagement)

Commenting on others' posts before your own goes live boosts initial reach by 20-30%. These rules govern AI-generated comments on other people's content. Comments are a different format from posts — they should read like a person talking, not a person writing.

**Voice shift:** Comments use the casual end of your voice range. Shorter sentences. More informal markers ("honestly," "yeah," "look"). Contractions always. No polished conclusions. The tone is "colleague at a coffee shop," not "thought leader on stage."

**Agreement calibration:** Opening with agreement is an AI tell. Not banned, but needs balance. Don't default to agreement as the opener — most AI-generated comments start with validation ("spot on," "this is so true," "great framing"). Prefer jumping straight into your own thought. If you agree, earn it: agreement is only worth stating if you're about to go somewhere specific with it. A comment that extends someone's idea already signals agreement implicitly. Across a session of 3-5 comments, no more than 1 should open with explicit agreement.

**Comment structure rules:**
- Length: 15-60 words. Long enough to add value, short enough to feel like a real comment. Never a mini-essay.
- Every comment must do ONE of these: (a) add a specific experience or example, (b) respectfully push back on one point, (c) extend the idea in a direction the author didn't go, (d) ask a genuine follow-up question
- Never just agree. Agreement without addition is noise.
- First person is mandatory. "I've seen..." not "One often finds..."

**Comment anti-patterns (banned):**
```
❌ "Couldn't agree more"
❌ "This resonates deeply"
❌ "Thanks for sharing this"
❌ "So true!" / "Love this"
❌ "Great post!" / "Great insight!"
❌ "Love this perspective"
❌ "Well said"
❌ Starting with the author's first name ("John, great point...")
❌ Mirror-back ("You're so right that X. I've also found X.")
❌ Forced humility ("I'm still learning but...")
❌ Emoji-only reactions
❌ Ending with "Keep it up!" or "More of this please"
❌ Any Tier 1 or Tier 2 banned vocabulary from Section 2.2
```

**How to find the angle:** The value of a comment is the angle the author didn't cover. Don't restate their point more eloquently. Start where the value starts.
1. Read the post. Identify the core claim.
2. Ask: what did they leave out? What's the next layer? What's the exception?
3. Lead with that. The agreement is implicit in the fact that you're engaging.

Three patterns that work:
- **New angle** — they made a good point but there's a dimension they missed. Lead with the missing dimension.
- **Reframe** — their point is right but there's a different way to see it. Put a new lens on it.
- **Respectful pushback** — disagree on one specific thing, not the whole comment.

What NOT to do: don't restate their point in your words (mirror-back), don't warm up with agreement before getting to your actual thought, don't compliment their framing as your opening move ("great way to put it," "spot on," "nailed it"). If you can delete the first sentence and the comment still works, delete it.

**Comment quality test:** Read the comment out loud. Does it sound like something you'd actually say to this person if you ran into them at a conference? If it sounds like a LinkedIn comment, rewrite it.

**Structural variety:** No two comments in the same session may use the same opening pattern. If the first comment starts with "I've seen this...", the second cannot start with "I've seen..." Rotate between:
- Leading with a specific example ("At a project last year...")
- Leading with a pushback ("I'd push back on one thing...")
- Leading with a question ("Curious whether...")
- Leading with an extension ("This connects to something I keep thinking about...")
- Leading with a sharp agreement on ONE specific point ("The bit about X is spot on. In my experience...")

**Targeting rules (which posts to comment on):**
- Prioritize accounts with 2x-10x your follower count (visibility multiplier without being invisible in a huge thread)
- Stay within your content pillars. Don't comment on posts outside your declared expertise.
- Prioritize posts under 2 hours old (the author's audience is still active, your comment gets seen)
- Sweet spot: posts with 5-30 comments (active conversation, not buried)
- Skip posts with 100+ comments (yours won't be seen)
- Skip posts where you have nothing genuine to add. Silence is better than a forced comment.

**Timing rules (pattern avoidance):**
- Space comments 3-8 minutes apart (never back-to-back)
- Vary the pre-post window: sometimes 60 minutes before your post, sometimes 30, sometimes 2 hours. Never the same pattern two days in a row.
- Maximum 5 comments per session. More than that looks automated regardless of quality.
- Never comment and post within the same 5-minute window.
- **APAC calibration:** If you're targeting cross-timezone audiences (e.g., ANZ posting for US/UK readers), relax the "post under 2 hours old" targeting rule to 12 hours. Active threads in distant timezones are still valuable even if posted hours ago.

**Pillar alignment:** Comments should reinforce your topical authority. If your pillars are product design, fractional leadership, and AI + design, your comments should consistently add value in those domains. A comment history that matches the posting history tells the algorithm "this person is a genuine participant in this topic."

---

## 5. Format & First Comment Rules

### 5.1 Format Diversity (Batch-Level, Mandatory)

Text + single image is the lowest-engagement native format on LinkedIn (3.85% avg). A monthly batch of 16 posts that are all text + image will consistently underperform a mixed batch.

**Monthly format mix rules:**

| Format | Avg engagement benchmark | Best use case |
|--------|--------------------------|---------------|
| Text + image | 3.85% | Quick insights, opinions, stories |
| Carousel / PDF document | 5.85-6.60% | Step-by-step guides, frameworks, myth-busting |
| Multi-image (2-5 images) | 6.60% | Before/after, process breakdown, tool comparison |
| Poll | +206% reach | Conversation starters, audience research |
| Video | 5.60% | Personal stories, hot takes, behind-the-scenes |

**Carousel generation rules:**
- File format: PDF only (LinkedIn dropped image carousel support Dec 2023)
- Optimal length: 6-10 slides
- Slide 1: Hook headline + visual indicator to swipe ("Swipe" or arrow)
- Each slide: One idea, large readable text, consistent design
- Final slide: CTA appropriate to your LinkedIn Goal (not "Follow me for more")
- Design: Uses your brand colour and consistent typography
- Dimensions: 1080x1350 (portrait) preferred for mobile dominance

### 5.2 First Comment (Mandatory for Every Post)

Every post must include a companion first comment to be published within 2 minutes of the main post. The first comment serves multiple purposes without cluttering the post body.

**First comment types (select based on post content and your goal):**

| Type | When to use | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Link drop** | Post references an article, tool, study, or resource | "Link to the study mentioned above: [URL]" |
| **Extended context** | Post tells a story that has useful backstory | "For context: this was during a complete platform rebuild. The team had gone from 24 people to 5 in three months." |
| **Follow-up thought** | Post makes a strong point that has a natural "and also..." | "One thing I didn't mention: the 43% dropout wasn't random. 80% of them left at the same field — 'company size.' They were solo founders." |
| **Soft CTA** | Your goal involves conversion (leads, launches, community) | "If this resonated and you want to talk through your own product's bottleneck, my DMs are open." |
| **Hashtag placement** | Post benefits from topic discovery | "Relevant if you're in #ProductDesign #SaaS #B2BMarketing" |

**Rules:**
- The first comment must add value. Never restate the post.
- Soft CTAs are limited to max 2 per week (4 per month). More than that reads as selling.
- Link drops should include a 1-sentence description, not just the URL.
- If the post references data or a source, the first comment MUST include the source link.

**Goal-specific first comment guidance:**

| LinkedIn Goal | Preferred first comment type | Notes |
|---------------|------------------------------|-------|
| Inbound leads | Soft CTA (2/week max) + Follow-up thought | CTA should feel like an offer, not a pitch. |
| Thought leadership | Extended context + Link drop | Shows intellectual depth beyond the post. |
| Grow audience | Follow-up thought + Hashtag placement | Hashtags aid discovery. Follow-up sparks secondary engagement. |
| Attract investment | Extended context | Investors want to see how you think, not be sold to. |
| Employer brand | Follow-up thought | Mention team, culture, or "we're hiring" occasionally. |
| Custom goal | Follow-up thought + Extended context | Default fallback. Adds depth without assuming intent. Adjust after first month's data. |

### 5.3 Image Strategy Rules

Every post with an image must follow these rules. The image is the first thing users see in the feed, it determines whether they stop scrolling.

**Rule 1: Images must support the post, not decorate it.**
- If the post tells a specific story, the image should relate to the story's context (industry, product type, scenario)
- If the post is a framework or how-to, the image should visualize the key concept or structure
- Generic abstract/gradient hero images with no connection to the content are prohibited. They signal AI generation and add no value.

**Rule 2: Brand consistency over variety.**
- You should have a recognizable image template (consistent colour palette, typography style, layout structure) so your posts are identifiable in the feed without reading the name
- The template uses your brand colour
- Variety comes from the content within the template, not from changing the template itself

**Rule 3: Text on images must be readable at mobile feed size.**
- Maximum 8 words on a hero image
- Minimum font size: equivalent to 48pt at 1080px width
- High contrast between text and background (WCAG AA minimum)

**Rule 4: Alt-text is mandatory.**
- Every image must include descriptive alt-text for accessibility
- Alt-text should describe the visual content, not repeat the post text
- Format: "[What the image shows] — [context relevant to the post]"
- This is both an accessibility requirement and a LinkedIn SEO signal

**Rule 5: AI-generated images require human-pass check.**
- If the image is AI-generated, it must not contain: distorted text, extra fingers/limbs, impossible architecture, or other common AI artifacts
- The monthly review should flag any AI-generated images that received negative comments or significantly lower engagement than your average

### 5.4 Pre-Post Engagement Prompt

LinkedIn's algorithm favours accounts that engage with others' content before posting their own.

**The recommendation:**
- "Before your next post goes live, spend 10 minutes commenting on 3-5 posts from people in your industry. Substantive comments (10+ words) on larger accounts boost your post's initial reach by 20-30%."
- This is a behavior recommendation for you, not something the system does on your behalf.

**Why this matters:**
- If you follow this advice you will see measurably better post performance
- If you don't, you'll still get value, but your results will be lower
- The monthly review should track: "Did you engage before posting this month? Your engagement rate was X% vs Y% industry average."

---

## 6. What Good Looks Like

### Signals of authentic content (aim for these)

- **Specific details the AI couldn't have invented:** "$47K budget, 23 interviews, 3 months"
- **Sentence variety:** Mix short fragments with longer flowing sentences
- **Tone shifts within the post:** Direct here, uncertain there, casual then precise
- **Actual position taken:** Not "balanced" — states what they believe and why
- **Real friction:** Problems without tidy resolutions
- **Language they'd use in conversation:** Not polished, not sloppy — natural
- **Scene-setting with genuine detail:** "The board meeting ran 90 minutes over..."
- **Starts with something specific:** A number, a scene, a statement — never a generalisation

### Content types that outperform in 2026

1. **First-person stories with specific numbers** — highest dwell time and save rate
2. **Original observations from real work** — unique to the poster, not repackaged advice
3. **Nuanced industry commentary with a position taken** — drives substantive comments
4. **Practical frameworks with examples** — saves-worthy, reference-worthy

### What the algorithm rewards

- **Dwell time** — users stopping to read fully, expanding "See more"
- **Meaningful comments (15+ words)** — industry dialogue, not "Great post!" Outweigh saves in algorithmic weight.
- **Saves** — 200 saves drives more reach than 1,000 likes
- **All comments** — even short ones signal engagement, but meaningful ones carry far more weight
- **Reply chains** — the author responding to commenters
- **Profile-content alignment** — consistent topics for 60-90+ days
- **Native formats** — no external links in body (25-40% reach reduction for links)

---

## 7. Hashtag Strategy

### 7.1 Hashtag Count and Placement

- **In the post body:** 0 hashtags (preferred) or maximum 2 if highly relevant to topic discovery
- **In the first comment:** 2-4 hashtags if using "hashtag placement" first comment type
- **Never:** More than 5 hashtags total across post + first comment. LinkedIn's algorithm treats this as spam signal since 2025.

### 7.2 Hashtag Selection Rules

**Volume targeting:** Choose hashtags in the 10K-500K post range (mid-volume). High-volume hashtags (#leadership, #marketing) are noise, your post drowns. Low-volume hashtags (#CryptoUXDesignNZ) have no audience.

**Relevance over reach:** Every hashtag must describe the actual content of the post, not your general industry. A post about support ticket reduction should use #CustomerExperience and #SaaS, not #DesignThinking.

**Rotation rules:**
- Never use the exact same hashtag set on consecutive posts
- Maintain a roster of 15-20 approved hashtags for your niche
- Rotate through 3-4 per post from the roster
- Review and refresh the roster quarterly based on which hashtags correlated with above-average reach

### 7.3 Hashtag Anti-Patterns

```
❌ #Hashtag at the end of every sentence
❌ Hashtags embedded mid-sentence for #emphasis
❌ Branded hashtags nobody follows
❌ Trending hashtags unrelated to the content (#SuperBowl on a design post)
❌ The word "hashtag" written out before the tag (LinkedIn's own formatting artifact — clean it)
```

---

## 8. Performance Feedback Loop

The system must learn from each month's data to improve the next month's content. Without this, the workflow generates based on static rules rather than what actually works for you.

### 8.1 Monthly Performance Analysis

After each monthly cycle, run an analysis across all posts from that month:

**Metrics collected per post:**
- Reactions count (broken down by type if available)
- Comment count
- Repost count
- Total engagement (reactions + comments + reposts)
- Impressions (if available via official API or self-reported)
- Engagement rate (engagement / impressions, if impressions available)

**Pattern extraction:**
- Which hook style got the most engagement? (story, contrarian, number, question)
- Which closing type drove the most comments? (Type A, B, or C)
- Which content pillar performed best?
- Which post length range performed best? (under 200, 200-350, 350-500, over 500 words)
- Which posting day/time performed best?
- Did any format outperform others? (text+image vs poll vs carousel)

### 8.2 Weight Adjustment Rules

Based on the monthly analysis, adjust generation weights for the next month:

**Rules:**
- Weight adjustments require a minimum sample of 3+ posts of the same type before acting. One outlier (viral or dead) should not shift the strategy.
- If a hook style outperforms your average by 50%+ across 3+ samples, increase its frequency by 1 post next month (e.g., from 3 story hooks to 4)
- If a hook style underperforms by 50%+ across 3+ samples, decrease its frequency by 1 post
- If a closing type drives 2x+ more comments than others, make it the default for 50% of next month's posts
- If a specific topic within a pillar consistently outperforms, weight it higher in topic selection
- Never let any single hook style or closing type exceed 65% of the monthly batch (prevents over-optimization while allowing room for data-driven defaults)
- Changes are gradual: max 2 weight shifts per month to avoid thrashing

### 8.3 Monthly Performance Report

Produce a monthly performance report that includes:

```
## Monthly Performance Summary — [Month Year]

### How Your LinkedIn Performed This Month

[Plain English summary, 3-5 sentences. Written conversationally, not as a data dump.
Explains what happened, why it happened, and what it means for next month.]

### The Numbers

| Metric | This Month | Last Month | Change |
|--------|-----------|------------|--------|
| Total engagements | [X] | [Y] | [+/-Z%] |
| Average engagements per post | [X] | [Y] | [+/-Z%] |
| Average comments per post | [X] | [Y] | [+/-Z%] |
| New followers | [+X] | [+Y] | [+/-Z%] |
| Profile views | [X] | [Y] | [+/-Z%] |
| Post views (weekly avg) | [X] | [Y] | [+/-Z%] |

### Top Performers
- Best post: [title/hook] — [X] engagements, [Y] comments
- Best hook style: [type] — [X]% above average
- Best closing type: [type] — [X]x more comments than others

### Underperformers
- Weakest post: [title/hook] — [X] engagements
- Pattern identified: [what went wrong — no question, off-pillar, too long, etc.]

### What We Changed Based on the Data

[Plain English explanation of what was learned and what's changing.
Not "adjusting hook style weights" — instead: "Personal stories consistently
outperform opinion posts. Next month, adding one more personal story
and replacing one opinion post."]

- [Specific change 1 in plain English]
- [Specific change 2 in plain English]
- [Specific change 3 in plain English]

### Competitive Update

[What was learned from analyzing top performers in your niche this month.]

### Your Engagement Habits
- You replied to [X]% of comments within 1 hour (target: 80%+)
- You engaged before posting on [X] of [Y] days (target: every posting day)
- Pre-post engagement correlated with [X]% higher reach on those days

### Next Month Preview
[Brief preview of next month's content themes and any experiments planned.]
```

**Writing rules for the monthly report:**
- The "How Your LinkedIn Performed" and "What We Changed" sections are the most important. They must read like a human strategist wrote them, not a dashboard.
- Never use jargon. "Hook style weights" becomes "which openings work best." "Pillar distribution" becomes "the mix of topics."
- Lead with the insight, not the number. "Your stories outperform your opinions" is better than "Personal story pillar: 8.4 avg engagement vs opinion pillar: 3.2 avg engagement."
- If something didn't work, say so plainly. "The AI trust post underperformed. We think it's because the topic was too technical for your audience. Adjusting."
- The Competitive Update should feel like intelligence, not surveillance. "Here's what we noticed" not "we scraped 10 accounts."
- Total report length: 2 pages maximum when rendered, scannable in under 3 minutes.

### 8.4 Weekly Preview Email

Sent every Monday morning before the week's posts go live. This is an **informational preview**, not an approval gate. The product promise is autopilot: posts publish on schedule unless you actively edit.

```
Subject: Your LinkedIn this week — [Month Day]-[Day]

Hey [First Name],

### This Week's Posts

[For each post, show:]

**[Day] — [Post pillar label]**
[First 120 characters of the post hook]...
[Image thumbnail if applicable]

→ [View full post + edit](link-to-review-page)

---

### Why These Posts

Based on: [Pillar name] · Audience pain point: [Y] · Trending in [Z] this week
[1-2 sentences of strategic context.]

### Worth Sharing?

[If applicable — passive suggestion, not a to-do]
We spotted a post worth sharing: "[Hook preview]" by [Author Name]
[1 sentence — why it fits your audience + draft commentary preview]

→ [Preview reshare](link-to-review-page)

---

[Looks good] · [Let me edit something]

Silence = autopilot. Posts go live as scheduled unless you edit.
```

**Writing rules for the weekly email:**
- This email is informational, not a gate. No checklists, no to-do items, no homework.
- Hook previews only (first 120 chars). Full post text lives on the review page, not in the email.
- The "Why These Posts" section uses Topic Attribution format: Pillar · Pain point · Trend. Never skip it.
- Reshare suggestions are passive ("We spotted..."), not action items.
- CTA structure: "Looks good" / "Let me edit something."
- Silence = autopilot. If you don't interact, posts publish as scheduled.
- Maximum length: scannable in under 2 minutes.

### 8.5 Re-Engagement Rules for Low Performers

When a post underperforms, follow this recovery guidance:

**Trigger:** A post has 0 comments after 4 hours AND total reactions are below your trailing 30-day average.

**Recovery actions:**
1. "Your post isn't getting traction yet. Three things you can do now:"
   - Comment on 5 posts from larger accounts in your niche (boosts your next algorithmic cycle)
   - Add a first comment with additional context or a follow-up insight (re-triggers the algorithm)
   - Share the post to your story with a brief "hot take" addition
2. If the post has 0 comments after 24 hours, edit the post to add a closing question (LinkedIn allows edits without resetting distribution)

**System learning:** Tag the post as "low performer" and extract what was different (hook type, closing, length, pillar, time, format) to avoid repeating the pattern.

---

## 9. Implementation Notes

### System Prompt Structure

```
SYSTEM PROMPT =
  [Base instructions + Ground Rules (Section 1)]
  + [Anti-Pattern Blacklist (Section 2) — as explicit DO NOT rules]
  + [Engagement Architecture (Section 4) — closing rules, see-more engineering, dwell time, hook-to-close coherence]
  + [Format & First Comment (Section 5) — format type for this post, first comment type, image rules]
  + [Hashtag Strategy (Section 7) — hashtag selection and placement for this post]
  + [Brand profile injection — positioning, voice, pillars, LinkedIn Goal]
  + [Voice Profile injection — slider/selector values + avoid list + em dash calibration]
  + [Content calendar context — this post's pillar, topic, format, position in monthly sequence, pillar weights]
  + [Reference samples — 3-5 approved posts that represent your voice]
  + [LinkedIn best practices — hook formulas, algorithm insights]
  + [Performance feedback — last month's weight adjustments if available (Section 8)]

USER PROMPT =
  "Write a [format] post on [topic] using pillar [X], hook style [Y], closing type [A/B/C].
   Include: (1) first comment (type: [link/context/thought/cta/hashtag]),
   (2) at least 1 reply template."
  + [Story input if provided]
  + [LinkedIn Goal for closing and first comment parameterization]
```

### Validation Pipeline (post-generation)

Validation runs in two passes to keep per-post calls cheap and batch checks efficient.

**Pass 1: Per-Post Validation (runs after each post is generated)**

```
PER-POST VALIDATION PROMPT =
  "Review this LinkedIn post against the following quality gates.
   For each gate, output PASS or FAIL with explanation.
   If any gate fails, rewrite the post to pass all gates."
  + [The generated post + first comment + reply templates]
  + [Gates 1-3, 5-6, 8 from Section 3]
  + [Your Voice Profile for Gate 3]
  + [Your LinkedIn Goal for Gate 6]
  + [Hook-to-close coherence check (Section 4.5)]
  + [Hashtag validation (Section 7)]
```

**Pass 2: Batch-Level Validation (runs once after all posts in the weekly batch are generated)**

```
BATCH VALIDATION PROMPT =
  "Review this weekly batch of posts against the following batch-level gates.
   For each gate, output PASS or FAIL with explanation.
   If any gate fails, identify which posts to rewrite and how."
  + [All 4 posts in the weekly batch with first comments and reply templates]
  + [Gates 4, 7, 9 from Section 3]
  + [Your content pillars for Gate 9]
  + [Pillar distribution targets (Section 4.8)]
  + [Format diversity requirements (Section 5.1)]
  + [Closing type variety rule (Section 4.1 batch rule)]
```

### Weekly Batch Variety

When generating the week's content (4 posts):
- Generate posts sequentially, passing previous posts as context
- Explicitly instruct: "The previous posts this week used hooks [X, Y]. Use a different hook."
- Rotate pillars deliberately: at least 2 different pillars per week
- Vary post length: some 150 words, some 300 words, max 1 at 500+ per week (never below 150-word minimum from Section 4.3)
- Track monthly variety across weekly batches: after week 4, run cumulative audit

---

## 10. Competitive Intelligence Framework

Every brand operates in a niche with established top performers. Analyze those performers, extract what works, determine what's transferable at your follower count, and feed those patterns back into content generation. This is the difference between generating posts from static rules and generating posts calibrated to what actually performs in your specific market.

**The core insight:** The analysis process is the same for everyone. The results are unique to each brand.

**Cross-reference:** Section 8.3's Competitive Update section depends on this framework.

### 10.1 Niche Benchmark Analysis (Runs at Onboarding, Refreshed Monthly)

**At onboarding, identify 5-10 benchmark accounts:**

Selection criteria:
- Same industry or adjacent (design leaders for a designer, founders for a founder)
- Mix of follower tiers: 2-3 accounts at 5K-50K (reachable peers), 2-3 at 50K-500K (aspirational targets), 1-2 at 500K+ (pattern sources, not direct comparators)
- Active posters (minimum 3 posts/week)
- You may suggest accounts, but the system should also discover accounts via keyword search and "similar profiles" signals

**For each benchmark account, pull the last 20-30 posts and extract:**

| Metric | What It Tells Us |
|--------|-----------------|
| Average reactions per post | Baseline for that account's reach |
| Average comments per post | Conversation-driving ability |
| Repost ratio (reposts / reactions) | Shareability of content |
| Reaction type breakdown (% Insightful, % Empathy, % Like) | Audience response mode: intellectual vs emotional vs passive |
| Post length distribution (word count ranges) | What length their audience tolerates |
| Hook style frequency | Which patterns they use and which perform |
| Closing style (question / statement / nudge / debate invitation) | What drives their comments |
| Link in body frequency | Whether they can afford the algorithm penalty |
| Format distribution (text / image / video / carousel / poll) | What formats their audience prefers |
| Hashtag strategy | Count, placement, specificity |
| Posting frequency and timing | Volume and schedule |

### 10.2 Pattern Extraction and Transferability Filter

Not every pattern from a 700K-follower account works for a 1K-follower account. Filter patterns through a transferability matrix.

**Transferability rules:**

| Pattern | Transferable at <5K followers? | Notes |
|---------|-------------------------------|-------|
| Personal stories with vulnerability | Yes | Works at any follower count. Often works BETTER at small counts because the audience is closer. |
| Data-backed opinions | Yes | Credibility is independent of follower count. |
| Debate invitations ("convince me I'm wrong") | Yes | Comment-driving patterns are follower-agnostic. |
| Coined terms / named concepts | Yes, if original | Creating vocabulary positions you as a thinker regardless of audience size. |
| Product announcements | No | Only works if you ARE the product (e.g., a Google engineer announcing a Google product). |
| Links in body | No | Algorithm penalty is proportionally worse at small follower counts. Always first comment. |
| Broad hashtags (#ai #programming) | No | At small counts, niche hashtags outperform. Switch to broad once above 10K. |
| Tagging thought leaders | Yes, carefully | Tag only when referencing their specific work. Don't tag-spam. At small counts, a tag from a larger account is disproportionately valuable. |
| Video-led posts | Conditional | Only if you create video. Never fabricate a format you can't sustain. |
| High posting frequency (daily) | No | At small counts, quality > quantity. 3-4/week is the sweet spot until 5K. |
| Carousel/poll format behavior | Scale-dependent | Carousels outperform at 5K+ where algorithmic distribution amplifies swipes. Under 5K, text + image still wins. Polls drive comments at any scale but have diminishing novelty. |
| Comment reply rate as engagement multiplier | Yes, amplified at small scale | At <5K, the author replying to every comment creates a disproportionate signal. At 50K+, selective replies are necessary due to volume. |
| Organic repost dynamics | No (<5K) | Organic reposts are rare under 5K regardless of quality. Don't optimize for repost-ability until your audience is large enough to generate them. |
| Posting time optimization | Conditional | Under 5K, posting time matters less than content quality. Above 10K, peak-hour posting compounds reach meaningfully. |

**Generate a "Transferable Patterns Report" at onboarding:**

```
## Competitive Intelligence — [Your Brand]

### Benchmark Accounts
[List of 5-10 accounts with follower count and niche]

### Top Patterns (Transferable)
1. [Pattern] — seen in [X] of [Y] top posts across benchmarks. Transferable because [reason].
2. [Pattern] — ...

### Patterns to Avoid (Not Transferable at Current Scale)
1. [Pattern] — works for [Account] at [X]K followers because [reason]. Not viable at [your count] because [reason].

### Recommended Strategy Adjustments
- [Specific change to make based on competitive data]

### Reaction Type Insight
[What the benchmark reaction breakdowns tell us about the niche audience:
 - High Insightful % = audience wants to learn, lean into teaching posts
 - High Empathy % = audience connects emotionally, lean into personal stories
 - High Like-only % = audience is passive, need stronger conversation triggers]
```

### 10.3 Monthly Competitive Refresh

Each month, alongside your own performance review (Section 8), pull fresh data from 3-5 benchmark accounts (rotate through the full list) and check:

1. **Has a new pattern emerged?** (e.g., a benchmark account started using carousels and their engagement doubled)
2. **Have you crossed a transferability threshold?** (e.g., crossed 5K, now broad hashtags become viable)
3. **Are any benchmark patterns you adopted working or failing?** Cross-reference: if you tried debate invitations last month, did your debate posts outperform?

**Output:** A "Competitive Update" section appended to the monthly report (Section 8.3):

```
### Competitive Update
- [Benchmark account] started [pattern], engagement up [X]%. Consider testing.
- [Pattern tried last month] performed [above/below] your average. [Keep/adjust/drop].
- Your follower count is now [X]. [New pattern] is now transferable.
```

---

## 11. Reshare Strategy

Resharing viral or high-performing content from larger accounts with original commentary is a proven visibility multiplier, especially for accounts under 5K followers. A reshare from a 500K-follower account exposes you to that account's audience. But reshares must be strategic, not random.

### 11.1 Reshare Scout

Reshare candidates are identified during the monthly content planning session and surfaced as passive suggestions in the weekly preview.

**Candidate selection criteria:**

| Criterion | Threshold | Why |
|-----------|-----------|-----|
| Original post reactions | 50+ and climbing | Indicates the post has momentum |
| Post age | Under 48 hours | LinkedIn's algorithm favors fresh reshares |
| Original poster follower count | 5K+ | Resharing from larger accounts multiplies reach |
| Topic alignment | Within your declared pillars | Off-topic reshares dilute positioning |
| Comment count | 5-100 (sweet spot) | Too few = not proven. Too many = your reshare gets lost. |
| Not already reshared by you recently | No reshare from same account in last 14 days | Avoids looking like a fan account |

**Discovery method:**
- Pull recent posts from your benchmark accounts (Section 10.1)
- Filter by the criteria above
- Rank by engagement velocity (reactions per hour since posting)
- Present top 2-3 with pre-drafted commentary

### 11.2 Reshare Commentary Rules

Reshare commentary follows the same voice rules as posts (Sections 2-4) with these modifications:

**Structure:**
- Length: 50-120 words (shorter than a full post, longer than a comment)
- Must take a clear position on the original content: agree with a specific reason, disagree with nuance, or extend with your own experience
- Must stand alone: if the reshared post were removed, the commentary should still make sense as a statement
- Must NOT just summarize or praise the original post

**What works:**
- "Here's what this looks like on the [your domain] side" — translating the insight to your niche
- "I've seen this firsthand" + specific example — adding a personal data point
- "I'd push back on one thing" — respectful disagreement drives comments on the reshare

**What doesn't work:**
- "Great post by [name]!" — adds nothing
- Restating the original post's argument in different words — redundant
- Commentary that only makes sense if you've read the original — not standalone

**Voice calibration:** Reshare commentary sits between post voice and comment voice. More casual than a post, more structured than a comment. Conversational markers ("honestly," "look," "the truth is") are encouraged. Short declarative sentences work well.

**Anti-patterns:**
- All anti-patterns from Section 2 apply to reshare commentary
- Additional: never reshare with just a quote from the original post. That's not commentary, that's highlighting.

### 11.3 Reshare Frequency and Placement

**Rules:**
- Maximum 2 reshares per month (Section 2.4 is canonical). Phase 2: increase to 1/week once you have reshare performance data.
- Caps: 1 reshare per week for accounts under 5K followers, 2 reshares per week for accounts 5K-50K.
- Never reshare on the same day as a pillar post. Reshares go on non-posting days (Monday, Saturday) or on the lightest posting day of the week.
- A reshare counts as content for algorithm purposes but does NOT count toward your pillar distribution (Section 4.8). It's supplementary.
- Reshares should alternate between benchmark tiers: one week reshare a peer (5K-50K), next week reshare a larger voice (500K+). This signals to the algorithm that you participate across the niche, not just follow one account.
- **Cadence interaction rule:** At 4x/week posting + weekly reshare = 5 posts/week total. This is at the documented quality dilution threshold (Section 4.7). Warn or auto-adjust cadence when reshare + posts exceed 5/week.

**Tagging rule:** Include the original author's name as plain text in reshare commentary. Manually @ tag them before the reshare publishes. The tag notification increases the chance they engage with your reshare, which brings their audience.

**Tracking:** Log each reshare with: original post URN, original author, your commentary, engagement received. Monthly review (Section 8) should compare reshare engagement vs original post engagement to determine if reshares are worth continuing at the current frequency.
